name: Mokele-Mbembe
description: Mokele-Mbembe, meaning 'one who stops the flow of rivers' in the Lingala language, is a cryptid said to inhabit the Congo River basin, particularly the Likouala swamp region. Descriptions vary, but it's generally depicted as a large, herbivorous sauropod-like creature. It is often described as having smooth, brownish-gray skin, a long, flexible neck, a small head, a long tail, and a body resembling that of an elephant or hippopotamus, but with a much longer neck and tail. Some accounts mention a single tooth or horn, while others deny its existence completely. Reports of Mokele-Mbembe date back centuries, with indigenous peoples sharing stories of the creature’s power and presence. Scientific expeditions have attempted to find conclusive evidence of its existence, but none have been successful. The creature holds a significant place in local folklore, often viewed with a mixture of fear and respect. It's considered a guardian of the rivers and swamps, and its alleged presence highlights the rich biodiversity and unexplored areas of the Congo basin. The Mokele-Mbembe remains a fascinating enigma, fueling both scientific curiosity and cultural narratives.
subtitle: The Congo's River-Stopping Cryptid
appearance: A large, sauropod-like creature with smooth, brownish-gray skin. It possesses a long, flexible neck, a small head, and a long tail. The body is often described as resembling that of an elephant or hippopotamus, but with a much longer neck and tail. Some accounts mention a single tooth or horn.
habitat: The Congo River basin, particularly the Likouala swamp region in the Republic of the Congo.
behavior: Generally believed to be herbivorous and peaceful unless provoked. It is said to be territorial and protective of its habitat. Accounts suggest it avoids human contact, preferring to remain hidden in the dense vegetation and deep waters of the swamps and rivers.

keyMyths.title: River Guardian
keyMyths.description: Mokele-Mbembe is seen as a guardian of the rivers and swamps, protecting the ecosystem from harm. Disturbing its habitat is said to bring misfortune to those who do so, according to local beliefs.

sources.author: Roy Mackal
sources.description: Mackal's book details his expeditions in search of Mokele-Mbembe and presents evidence gathered from interviews with local people.
sources.text: Mackal's book details his expeditions in search of Mokele-Mbembe and presents evidence gathered from interviews with local people.

sources.author: Herman Regusters
sources.work: Mokele-Mbembe: Mystery Beast of the Congo
sources.description: Regusters's work documents his expedition and provides photographic evidence (though disputed) of the creature.
sources.text: Regusters's work documents his expedition and provides photographic evidence (though disputed) of the creature.
